findings.title: Kubernetes Secrets stored in Helm values with base64-encoded placeholders committed to Git
findings.description: Task 1005 explicitly states: 'Use Helm --set or values file with base64-encoded placeholders; document that real values must be injected via CI/CD or a sealed-secrets workflow before production use' and instructs committing a values-secrets.yaml.example showing required keys. Base64 encoding is not encryption. If developers ever populate this file with real values and commit it, or if the example file is used carelessly, all nine API secrets (Stripe, OpenAI, Twilio, LinkedIn, Google, ElevenLabs, Cloudflare R2, JWT) would be exposed in the Git repository. The task does not mandate Sealed Secrets, External Secrets Operator, or any cryptographic protection as a hard requirement — it is only documented as an option.
findings.recommendation: Mandate a specific secret management approach before implementation. Either (a) adopt Bitnami Sealed Secrets with SealedSecret CRs committed to Git and a ClusterSealedSecretsController installed in the cluster, or (b) adopt External Secrets Operator backed by a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Cloudflare Workers KV). Remove the values-secrets.yaml.example pattern entirely. Add a git pre-commit hook or CI check (e.g., detect-secrets) that fails if any file containing STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY, or JWT_SECRET is staged for commit. The scale_tasks reference to dp-10-3 explicitly defers this to v2 — this must be reconsidered given the sensitivity of the secrets involved.

findings.title: GDPR internal endpoints protected only by X-Internal-Request header or weak X-Internal-Key — no cryptographic authentication
findings.description: Multiple services (tasks 2010, 3011, 4012, 5007, 6014) implement GDPR export and delete endpoints at /internal/gdpr/* protected by checking for the presence of an HTTP header (X-Internal-Request or X-Internal-Key matched against an env var). The primary access control is described as 'enforced at network level via Cilium NetworkPolicy' with the header check as 'defense-in-depth.' However, Cilium policies are applied at the pod-to-pod level and if any permitted caller pod is compromised, it could invoke GDPR delete across all five services. The X-Internal-Key mechanism also lacks key rotation, expiry, or audit binding. Furthermore, the Morgan GDPR orchestration tools fan out to all five services — if Morgan's tool invocation is manipulated, it could trigger mass data deletion.
findings.recommendation: Replace the header-only authentication with mutual TLS (mTLS) between internal services, enforced via Cilium + SPIFFE/SPIRE for certificate issuance, or use short-lived signed JWTs with a dedicated 'gdpr-operator' audience claim. Add a mandatory human confirmation step in the Morgan admin skill prompt (already partially specified in 7004 but not enforced cryptographically). Implement rate limiting on GDPR delete endpoints (max 5 calls/hour per operator). Log every GDPR delete invocation with the operator identity, timestamp, and customer_id to the audit schema before executing deletions, not after.

findings.title: JWT secret shared across all services without key rotation or JWKS endpoint
findings.description: Tasks 2 (Rust equipment-catalog), 4 (Finance), 5 (Customer Vetting), and 6 (Social Engine) all use a single JWT_SECRET from the sigma1-jwt-secret Kubernetes Secret for HS256 token validation. This is a symmetric shared secret architecture. If the secret is compromised, all services are simultaneously vulnerable. There is no JWKS endpoint, no key rotation mechanism, no token revocation list, and no per-service audience validation. A token issued for one service is valid for all services. Task 4010 notes 'shared secret vs. public key from an identity service must be decided' but leaves it as a decision point without resolution.
findings.recommendation: Replace HS256 shared secret with RS256 asymmetric signing. Deploy a lightweight identity provider (Keycloak, Dex, or a custom JWKS endpoint) that issues tokens with per-service audience claims (e.g., 'aud': 'equipment-catalog-v1'). Each service validates the 'aud' claim. The public key is distributed via a JWKS endpoint; private key remains in the identity provider. This eliminates the blast radius of a single secret compromise. If HS256 must be used for v1, implement per-service secret keys and add a JWKS-compatible rotation mechanism.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint processes raw body after middleware deserialization risk
findings.description: Task 4005 correctly notes the requirement to 'read raw request body as bytes before any deserialization (required for HMAC validation)' for the Stripe webhook. However, this requirement conflicts with the JWT middleware applied in task 4010 which wraps all /api/v1/* routes. If the JWT middleware consumes or buffers the request body before the Stripe webhook handler reads it, HMAC validation will fail or the body will be double-read incorrectly. The task attempts to exclude the webhook path from JWT middleware but the exclusion logic must be precise — a misconfiguration could either (a) allow unauthenticated access to all /api/v1/* routes or (b) break Stripe signature validation.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly document and test the middleware ordering in Axum. The Stripe webhook route /api/v1/webhooks/stripe must be registered on a separate router branch that bypasses the JWT middleware entirely and uses a dedicated body extractor (axum::body::Bytes) before any middleware layer consumes the body. Add an integration test that verifies: (1) a request to /api/v1/webhooks/stripe with a valid Stripe-Signature but no JWT returns 200, (2) the HMAC validation uses the exact raw bytes Stripe signed (no JSON normalization). This test must be a CI gate.

findings.title: signal-cli container uses ':latest' image tag — no image pinning or vulnerability scanning
findings.description: Task 1006 deploys Signal-CLI using image 'signalapp/signal-cli:latest'. Using ':latest' tags in production is a supply chain security risk: the image content can change between deployments without notice, potentially introducing vulnerabilities or breaking changes. No container image scanning step is present in the CI/CD pipeline (tasks 10014, 10015 only reference code linting). The scale_tasks do not include image scanning. Additionally, the Signal-CLI image is from Docker Hub (not a trusted private registry), and the image has broad file system access to Signal registration credentials stored on the PVC.
findings.recommendation: Pin the signal-cli image to a specific digest: 'signalapp/signal-cli@sha256:<digest>'. Add Trivy or Grype container scanning to the CI pipeline as a required gate: 'trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL signalapp/signal-cli:<tag>'. Mirror approved images to a private registry (ghcr.io/sigma1/ or a Cloudflare-managed registry) and pull exclusively from there. Add an image policy admission controller (Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) that rejects pods using ':latest' tags or images from unregistered registries. Apply this policy to all namespaces including signal.

findings.title: Valkey (Redis) has no authentication configured for dev, with no enforced transition to production auth
findings.description: Task 1004 explicitly sets 'spec.redisConfig: no auth for dev' and notes 'document production auth requirement' as a comment. The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap stores the Valkey URL as 'redis://sigma1-valkey.databases.svc.cluster.local:6379' — no password in the connection string. Tasks 2006 (rate limiting), 4008 (currency rate caching), 5006 (vetting result caching), and 6014 (health checks) all connect to this unauthenticated Valkey instance. If any pod in the cluster is compromised, an attacker can read all cached data including vetting results (PII), rate limit counters (enabling bypass by direct deletion), and currency rates. There is no task in the plan that enables Valkey authentication before production (task 10 scale_tasks only scales replicas).
findings.recommendation: Enable Valkey/Redis authentication immediately, even in dev, to establish the pattern. Add 'requirepass' configuration to the Opstree Redis CR and store the password in a dedicated Kubernetes secret (sigma1-valkey-secret). Update the VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL in sigma1-infra-endpoints to include the password: 'redis://:password@sigma1-valkey.databases.svc.cluster.local:6379'. Add a Kyverno policy that blocks Valkey connections without TLS (if Redis 6+ TLS is available in the Opstree operator). Add this as a hard requirement in the production hardening task (10) with an explicit test: 'redis-cli -h <host> ping without auth returns NOAUTH error'.

findings.title: Morgan Kubernetes Deployment uses a single replica with no Signal-CLI state persistence guarantee
findings.description: Task 7008 and decision point dp-10-1 identify that Morgan is a single-replica Deployment with Signal-CLI state on a PVC. The decision point explicitly notes 'data loss risk on Morgan pod restart if Signal-CLI registration state is not persisted' but leaves the resolution open. If Morgan's pod is restarted (OOM kill, node drain, image update), Signal-CLI registration state may be lost if the PVC is not properly mounted or the Deployment is not using a StatefulSet with stable pod identity. Additionally, task 1006 provisions the signal-cli PVC in the 'signal' namespace separately from Morgan in 'openclaw', but task 7008 mounts '/workspace' from 'morgan-workspace' PVC — it is unclear if Signal-CLI registration data is persisted on this PVC or lost.
findings.recommendation: Convert Morgan to a StatefulSet with a dedicated PVC for Signal-CLI state. The signal-cli-data PVC (5Gi in the signal namespace) and the morgan-workspace PVC (10Gi in openclaw) should be reconciled — Signal-CLI state must be on a persistent volume with a stable mount path. Use a StatefulSet with volumeClaimTemplates to guarantee PVC rebinding on pod restart. Add a liveness probe that checks Signal-CLI JSON-RPC connectivity (not just Morgan's HTTP health). Add a documented recovery runbook for Signal re-registration that does not require manual kubectl exec.

findings.title: ArgoCD Application CRs use automated sync with prune: true — risk of accidental resource deletion
findings.description: Task 10013 configures all six ArgoCD Applications with 'automated: { selfHeal: true, prune: true }'. With prune: true, any resource present in the cluster but not in the Git repository will be automatically deleted by ArgoCD during sync. This is a double-edged configuration: while it enforces GitOps purity, it means a mistaken Git push (e.g., accidentally deleting a Helm template, or a Git repository corruption event) could cause ArgoCD to prune production Deployments, Services, or PersistentVolumeClaims. The plan does not include a pre-sync hook or a resource exclusion list to protect stateful resources (PVCs, CloudNative-PG cluster) from pruning.
findings.recommendation: Add resource exclusion rules to each ArgoCD Application to prevent pruning of critical stateful resources: PersistentVolumeClaims, CloudNative-PG Cluster CRs, and Valkey CRs. In the Application spec add: 'spec.syncPolicy.syncOptions: ["PrunePropagationPolicy=foreground", "PruneLast=true"]' and annotate critical resources with 'argocd.argoproj.io/managed-by-cluster-argocd: false' or use ArgoCD's resource exclusion config. Implement a two-step sync approval for destructive changes: automated sync for non-breaking changes, manual sync gate for Deployment deletions or replica count reductions.

findings.title: No centralized audit trail for administrative actions across services
findings.description: While GDPR delete operations write to an audit schema, there is no unified audit trail for administrative actions such as: admin JWT token usage on write endpoints (POST/PATCH catalog products), manual kubectl exec into pods, ArgoCD sync operations, secret access events, and RBAC role usage. Task 10011 creates ServiceAccounts with least privilege but does not enable Kubernetes audit logging. The GDPR audit entries are scattered across per-service audit tables (audit.gdpr_deletions in RMS task 3011, finance.gdpr_deletions in task 4002) rather than a single audit schema, making cross-service compliance reporting difficult.
findings.recommendation: Enable Kubernetes API server audit logging with a policy that captures all 'admin' level events (secret reads, pod exec, deployment modifications) and ships logs to a centralized Loki or OpenSearch instance. Consolidate all GDPR audit entries into a single 'audit' schema (already referenced in task 3011 but inconsistently used across services). Implement a structured audit log format: {timestamp, operator_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, service, outcome}. Add Prometheus alerting on unusual admin API call rates. For the Grafana dashboards in task 10, add an audit events panel showing GDPR operations, admin logins, and secret rotation events.

findings.title: Social engine accepts multipart file uploads with no MIME type validation or file size limits
findings.description: Task 6004 implements POST /api/v1/social/upload as a multipart Elysia route that 'validates content-type is multipart/form-data' but does not specify MIME type validation of individual uploaded files, maximum file size limits, or maximum number of files per request. The uploaded files are stored directly in R2 under events/{event_id}/source/{uuid}.jpg. An attacker with a valid JWT could upload non-image files (executables, HTML files for stored XSS if served directly) or extremely large files causing denial of service on the upload handler and storage costs. The AI curation pipeline then makes OpenAI vision API calls on these uploaded URLs, which could be exploited to inject adversarial prompts via image metadata.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit file validation in the upload handler: (1) check magic bytes (not just extension) to verify JPEG/PNG/WebP format using a library like 'file-type' for Node.js, (2) enforce maximum file size of 25MB per file and maximum 20 files per request, (3) sanitize file names before using them as R2 keys (already using UUID so this is partially addressed), (4) add Content-Type validation on individual file parts within the multipart body. Set S3/R2 bucket policy to enforce maximum object size. For the OpenAI vision calls, implement prompt injection detection by validating the model's JSON response schema strictly — reject any response where score fields are not numeric in range 0-10.

findings.title: Cloudflare Tunnel CORS configured as wildcard ('*') in Morgan web chat adapter
findings.description: Task 7007 configures the Morgan web chat adapter with 'cors_origins: ["*"]' and comments 'tighten to Sigma-1 domain post-launch.' This wildcard CORS policy means any website can make cross-origin requests to the Morgan chat endpoint (chat.sigma1.com/chat). Combined with the exposed chat endpoint through Cloudflare Tunnel, this allows any third-party site to embed a Morgan chat session, potentially harvesting conversation data, impersonating users across sessions, or abusing the OpenAI API calls (and associated costs) through the Morgan proxy.
findings.recommendation: Set CORS origins to the specific production domain list before the Cloudflare Tunnel is deployed externally: 'cors_origins: ["https://sigma1.com", "https://www.sigma1.com", "https://app.sigma1.com"]'. Add an Origin validation middleware that returns 403 for unexpected origins. Implement session-based rate limiting per origin to prevent API cost abuse. Add a Cloudflare WAF rule to block requests to /chat that do not originate from approved Referer headers. The 'post-launch tightening' note is a deferred security control that should be a pre-launch gate.

findings.title: Currency rate sync background task stores live rates in Valkey without encryption, and the external rate provider API key is not defined
findings.description: Task 4008 implements a currency rate sync that stores rate data in Valkey as plaintext JSON. While currency rates themselves are not PII, the pattern of storing sensitive data (the vetting results in task 5006 are cached the same way with PII) without encryption at rest establishes a poor precedent. More critically, the 'exchangerate.host or similar free API' is unresolved — this is a dependency where the specific provider, API key management, and reliability SLA are undefined. Using a free API for financial rate data introduces availability risk and potential for data manipulation if the provider is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Resolve the currency provider selection before implementation (mark as a hard dependency blocker, not just a decision point). Candidates: Open Exchange Rates (paid, reliable), Fixer.io, or European Central Bank XML feed (free, reliable for EUR base). Add the provider API key to the sigma1-finance-secret Kubernetes Secret. For Valkey caching of financial data, consider enabling Redis TLS if the Opstree operator supports it, or treat cached rates as non-sensitive but add a staleness check (if rates:v1 TTL is near zero, warn in health endpoint).

findings.title: No dependency auditing in CI pipeline for Go and Node.js services
findings.description: Task 10 scale_tasks and subtask 10015 specify 'Cipher runs Semgrep + cargo-audit + nancy (Go) + npm audit; blocks merge on CRITICAL/HIGH' but this is in the production hardening task (task 10), meaning all development in tasks 2-9 proceeds without automated dependency vulnerability scanning. The Go services (RMS, task 3) use multiple external dependencies (grpc-gateway, pgx, zap) that may have known CVEs. The Node.js social engine (task 6) uses numerous npm packages including sharp (native bindings) and multiple social API SDKs. The Rust services run cargo-audit only after the fact.
findings.recommendation: Move dependency auditing to the earliest CI step, not the final hardening task. Add to each service's initialization subtask (3001, 4001, 5001, 6001): (1) pin all dependency versions exactly (no caret ^ or tilde ~ ranges), (2) add a 'cargo audit' / 'npm audit --audit-level high' / 'govulncheck ./...' step to the service-level test task. Create a GitHub Actions reusable workflow 'dependency-audit.yml' that runs on PR open. Add Dependabot or Renovate bot configuration to auto-raise PRs for security patches in Go, Rust, and Node.js services.

findings.title: Morgan MCP tools authenticate to backend services without service-to-service auth tokens
findings.description: Tasks 7003 and 7004 define MCP tools that make HTTP calls to in-cluster service URLs (e.g., equipment-catalog-svc.sigma1.svc.cluster.local:8080). These calls do not include any authentication headers — no JWT, no API key, no mTLS. The backend services (tasks 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) all require JWT authentication on /api/v1/* endpoints. This means either: (a) Morgan must obtain and forward a valid JWT, which is not specified in any tool configuration, or (b) the backend services will return 401 for all Morgan tool calls in production. The tools.yaml has no auth configuration for service-to-service calls.
findings.recommendation: Define a service identity mechanism for Morgan's MCP tool calls. Options: (1) Morgan obtains a long-lived service JWT (role: 'agent', sub: 'morgan') at startup from the JWT_SECRET and includes it as Authorization: Bearer in all tool HTTP calls — configure this in tools.yaml as a default_auth block, (2) Use Kubernetes projected service account tokens with audience binding per service, (3) Add an 'agent' role to each service's JWT validation middleware that grants read/write access appropriate for Morgan's operations. Document the token refresh strategy to handle JWT expiry during long-running conversations.

findings.title: Database connection strings in ConfigMap include username but not TLS enforcement
findings.description: Task 1007 creates the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap with CNPG_SIGMA1_URL set to 'postgresql://sigma1_user@sigma1-postgres-rw.databases.svc.cluster.local:5432/sigma1'. The URL does not include TLS parameters (sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full). CloudNative-PG generates TLS certificates by default, but unless the connection string explicitly requires TLS, some clients may fall back to unencrypted connections. Given the cluster spans multiple namespaces (sigma1, databases, openclaw, signal), cross-namespace database traffic should be encrypted.
findings.recommendation: Append '?sslmode=require' to the CNPG_SIGMA1_URL in the ConfigMap. For production, use 'sslmode=verify-full' with the CNPG-generated CA certificate mounted as a volume. Update all service Deployment manifests to mount the CNPG CA secret as an environment variable or file. Add a test to the schema-init job that verifies the connection is using TLS: 'SELECT ssl FROM pg_stat_ssl WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid()' returns true.

findings.title: Prometheus /metrics endpoints are unauthenticated and accessible within cluster
findings.description: All five backend services (tasks 2008, 3010, 4011, 5007, 6014) expose GET /metrics in Prometheus text format. These endpoints are unauthenticated. While they are on ClusterIP services and not directly internet-accessible, the Cilium network policies (tasks 10004-10009) do not explicitly list Prometheus scraping as an allowed ingress flow. This means either the metrics endpoints are unreachable by Prometheus (breaking observability) or they require an overly permissive ingress rule. Additionally, Prometheus metrics can reveal internal application state (queue depths, error rates, DB query patterns) useful for reconnaissance.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit ingress rules in each CiliumNetworkPolicy to allow traffic from the Prometheus scraper pod (labeled app=prometheus or app=prometheus-server) on port 8080 (or the metrics port). Consider moving /metrics to a separate port (e.g., 9090) not exposed by the service ClusterIP to isolate it from application traffic. For sensitive environments, add Bearer token authentication to /metrics endpoints using the Prometheus scrape configuration 'bearer_token_file' mechanism.

strengths: Comprehensive GDPR deletion pipeline with per-service internal endpoints and audit schema logging (tasks 2010, 3011, 4012, 5007, 6014, 7004) — a strong privacy-by-design foundation that covers all five data-holding services.

Cilium NetworkPolicy coverage is thorough and service-specific (tasks 10004-10009), with explicit FQDN-based egress rules for each external API rather than open internet egress, which significantly reduces the blast radius of a compromised pod.

Defense-in-depth approach to GDPR endpoints using both Cilium NetworkPolicy (primary) and header-based access control (secondary), with audit logging — demonstrates security layering even if the authentication mechanism needs strengthening.

Container resource limits defined for all services (256Mi-512Mi RAM, 250m-500m CPU) with separate liveness and readiness probes, preventing OOM-driven security incidents and ensuring degraded dependencies are surfaced before traffic is routed.

Atomic database transactions for inventory checkout conflict detection (task 3006) using SELECT FOR UPDATE, and for payment recording (tasks 4003, 4004) — correctly preventing TOCTOU race conditions in financial operations.
Rate limiting implemented at the application layer (Valkey sliding window in task 2006) for all catalog API endpoints, reducing denial-of-service exposure on the most public-facing service.
Stripe webhook raw body HMAC validation is explicitly called out as a requirement (task 4005), demonstrating awareness of the common implementation mistake of deserializing before verifying.

All services implement /health/ready with dependency checks (postgres + valkey) separate from /health/live, enabling Kubernetes to remove pods from load balancing before they return incorrect results due to dependency failures.
summary: The Sigma-1 platform plan demonstrates strong security awareness in several areas (GDPR pipeline, network policy granularity, Stripe webhook handling) but carries high overall risk primarily due to three unresolved critical issues: the absence of a mandated secrets management strategy leaving nine API keys potentially exposed in Git, unauthenticated Valkey instances in all environments, and Morgan's MCP tools lacking service-to-service authentication that would cause 401 failures against all JWT-protected endpoints in production. The JWT architecture using a single shared HS256 secret across all five services represents a systemic authentication design gap that requires resolution before any service reaches production. Fourteen additional findings ranging from supply chain risks (unpinned signal-cli:latest image) to audit trail gaps and CORS misconfiguration require remediation, with the majority addressable through configuration changes rather than architectural rework.
